title: Falls Risk in Home Health: Understanding the HHVBP Falls Measure
description: How falls screening affects your HHVBP score. Understand CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing falls quality measures.

blocks.question: What is the HHVBP falls measure and how does it affect reimbursement?
blocks.directAnswer: The CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing model includes a falls measure that adjusts reimbursement based on your agency's falls incidence and assessment practices. Agencies with higher fall rates or inadequate screening receive payment reductions of up to 5% under the model beginning 2024.

tldrBullets.text: HHVBP falls measure is based on OASIS-C2 data, specifically items M1025 and M1027
Agencies must screen for falls risk at start-of-care and implement evidence-based interventions
Poor documentation or high fall rates trigger payment adjustments of 2-5% depending on performance tier

blocks.expandedAnswer:
The Home Health Value-Based Purchasing model is CMS's approach to linking Medicare payments to quality outcomes. Beginning 2024, the falls measure became a formal payment adjustment mechanism. Agencies are ranked by falls risk assessment completion rates and falls event documentation. Top performers (highest assessment rates, lowest falls incidence) receive 5% payment bonuses. Lower performers face 2-5% reductions.

The falls measure focuses on OASIS-C2 items M1025 and M1027. M1025 captures whether falls risk assessment was completed at start-of-care (SOC). M1027 captures the patient's risk level. If your agency has low completion rates for these items, you automatically fall into a lower performance tier, regardless of actual falls incidence.

Abel Care supports US agencies meeting these requirements. Our platform captures OASIS-required data, generates proper coding, and enables outcome tracking. This ensures compliance and positions you for HHVBP bonuses rather than penalties.

Understanding HHVBP is critical for home health business strategy. A $50M revenue agency facing 5% payment reduction loses $2.5M annually. Conversely, demonstrating excellence in falls prevention can secure bonus payments and competitive advantage.

sources.title: CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing Program
CDC STEADI: Screening and Assessment Recommendations

blocks.body:
What is HHVBP and how does it work

Home Health Value-Based Purchasing is CMS's national reporting system launched in 2016 and extended with financial penalties and bonuses from 2024 onwards. Rather than paying all agencies the same fixed rate per episode, CMS now adjusts payments based on quality metrics, including falls assessment and prevention.

The model has three tiers. The top 25% of agencies (by falls assessment completion and prevention outcomes) receive a 5% payment increase. The middle 50% receive standard rates. The bottom 25% face a 5% reduction. This creates strong financial incentive for agencies to prioritize falls prevention.

Payment adjustments are calculated by episode, affecting all claims submitted in a given year. A typical home health episode is 60 days. For a patient receiving multiple episodes (readmissions), each episode's OASIS coding affects the episode-specific payment. Over a year, agencies with consistent compliance and good outcomes accumulate financial advantage. Those with gaps accumulate penalties.

OASIS-C2 falls documentation requirements

OASIS-C2 is the mandatory assessment instrument for Medicare home health patients. Items M1025 and M1027 specifically address falls. M1025 asks: "Has the patient been screened for falls risk?" Responses are Yes or No. Completion is mandatory at start-of-care. If you answer "No", your agency is marked non-compliant on that episode.

M1027 follows: "What is the patient's risk level?" Options are: No score obtained, Low risk, Medium risk, High risk, or Unable to assess. This item requires documentation of your risk assessment methodology. You must specify which tool or approach you used (CDC STEADI questions, Berg Balance Scale, Timed Up and Go, multifactorial assessment, etc.).

HHVBP analytics use these data points to rank agencies. Completion rate is the first filter: agencies below 85% completion face automatic downgrades regardless of outcomes. Agencies above 85% completion are compared by risk distribution and fall events (documented via other OASIS items).

Documentation must be timely and complete. The assessment must occur during the first 48 hours of care, before the end of the start-of-care visit. Late assessments or missing data points trigger compliance flags. Use your electronic record system to flag when M1025 and M1027 are incomplete and notify clinicians immediately.

Compliance steps for home health agencies

Step 1: Audit current OASIS completion. Query your electronic health record (EHR) for the past 12 months of episodes. Calculate percentage of episodes with M1025 completed (documented Yes or No) and M1027 documented with a specific risk level. Most agencies discover 60-75% completion; HHVBP requires 85%+. Identify patterns: are certain clinicians non-compliant? Certain patient populations missed?

Step 2: Select a standardized falls risk assessment tool. Choose one approach consistently. Options include: (a) Two brief questions from CDC STEADI ("Have you had a fall in the past year?" and "Do you feel unsteady?"), (b) A formal tool like Berg Balance Scale, Timed Up and Go, or STRATIFY, (c) Multifactorial assessment (functional, medication, medical, environmental), or (d) A digital platform like Able Assess. Consistency matters: if clinicians use different approaches, coding becomes subjective and OASIS data becomes unreliable.

Step 3: Integrate screening into your SOC workflow. Modify your start-of-care visit documentation template to include falls risk assessment as a required field. Make it impossible to complete an SOC visit without documenting M1025 and M1027. Electronic workflows with mandatory fields are most effective.

Step 4: Develop risk-stratified interventions. Low-risk patients: general falls prevention education, ensure safe environment. Medium-risk: add home modifications (handrails, lighting), review medications. High-risk: urgent PT/OT referral, medication review with prescriber, consideration of emergency alert systems or increased visit frequency. Document interventions in the care plan.

Step 5: Train all clinicians. Nurses and therapists conducting SOC visits must understand OASIS items, the assessment tool selected, and documentation standards. Training should include: (a) when assessment must occur (within 48 hours), (b) how to use your selected tool, (c) how to code risk level appropriately, and (d) what interventions follow each risk level. New staff orientation must include this training.

Step 6: Implement tracking and accountability. Generate monthly reports: percentage of episodes with M1025/M1027 completed, breakdown by risk level, and follow-up intervention rates. Share with leadership and clinicians. Flag individuals or teams with poor compliance. Tie compliance to performance evaluations. This creates accountability.

Step 7: Monitor falls events. Track actual falls in your patient population using OASIS item M1028 (patient experienced falls since admission). Aim for <5% fall rate. Compare your rate to national benchmarks (typically 8-12% for home health). Higher-than-benchmark rates trigger interventions: review intervention fidelity, consider more intensive assessment, analyze fall patterns (common mechanisms, times, locations).

How Able Assess supports HHVBP compliance

Able Assess is designed specifically for home health agencies managing OASIS compliance. At the start-of-care visit, clinicians use Able Assess to complete a structured falls risk assessment. The platform guides through evidence-based screening questions, captures patient responses, and generates an OASIS-compatible risk rating.

Results are automatically integrated into your OASIS submission forms (M1025 marked "Yes", M1027 populated with the assessed risk level). This eliminates the manual data transfer step where errors often occur. Your clinical judgment is preserved: clinicians still perform the assessment and make risk determinations; Able Assess just automates the documentation and coding.

The platform tracks outcomes over time. You can generate monthly reports showing: percentage of episodes assessed, risk distribution (how many low/medium/high), intervention completion rates, and actual falls events. This data positions you for HHVBP bonus payments. You can also identify patterns (e.g., patients with specific diagnoses have higher fall rates) and adjust interventions accordingly.

Able Assess integrates with major home health EHRs (Medidata, Avantas, etc.), so assessment data flows automatically into your OASIS workflow. This reduces clinician administrative burden, improves accuracy, and ensures compliance.

stats.stat: Home health patients have 2-3 times higher fall risk than community-dwelling older adults
stats.source: CDC STEADI data on home health populations

stats.stat: Falls risk assessment completion rates among US home health agencies average 65-75%, well below HHVBP target of 85%
Agencies in top HHVBP quartile (85%+ assessment completion, <5% fall rate) receive 5% payment bonus
Falls prevention interventions reduce home health falls by 25-35%

blocks.body:
Common documentation errors and how to avoid them

Error 1: M1025 left blank or coded "Unable to assess." This is treated as non-compliance. Solution: Make M1025 mandatory in your EHR. It must always be "Yes" or "No". If a patient refuses assessment, code "Yes" and document refusal, then code M1027 as "Unable to assess" with explanation.

Error 2: M1027 coded without specification of tool or methodology. "High risk" alone is insufficient. Document: "High risk per CDC STEADI screening (positive to both questions) and Berg Balance Scale score of 25/56, indicating high fall risk." This specificity protects your agency during CMS audits.

Error 3: Assessment performed late (after day 2 of care or during later visit visits). OASIS requires SOC assessment. If your clinician forgets during the SOC visit and completes it during the second visit, you're non-compliant on that episode. Solution: Electronic reminder systems flag incomplete OASIS before the SOC visit is finalized.

Error 4: Assessment tool varies by clinician. One clinician uses Berg Balance Scale, another uses TUG, another uses just clinical judgment. This creates non-standardized data and unreliable OASIS coding. Solution: Mandate a single assessment tool organization-wide. Update all clinicians simultaneously. Audit compliance quarterly.

Error 5: Interventions not documented or not implemented. Assessment is completed and risk coded, but no documented care plan interventions follow. HHVBP reviewers note: "Coded high risk, no PT referral documented." This triggers questions about quality. Solution: Tie assessment to mandatory care planning. High-risk coding automatically generates an intervention checklist.

items.question: Does our agency have to participate in HHVBP?
items.answer: HHVBP is mandatory for all Medicare-participating home health agencies. If you accept Medicare, you submit OASIS data, and your data affects payment. You cannot opt out. However, you can control your performance: improve assessment rates and fall prevention outcomes to achieve bonuses rather than penalties.

items.question: How often should we reassess falls risk during a 60-day episode?
items.answer: OASIS requires formal assessment at start-of-care (M1025/M1027) and at discharge (required for episode closure). Interim reassessment is clinically indicated if patient has a fall, hospitalization, or change in function. Document interim reassessments in your clinical notes; they affect care planning decisions even if not formally recoded in OASIS.

items.question: What if we score many patients as high-risk? Does that hurt our HHVBP ranking?
items.answer: Not if your assessment is accurate and you implement appropriate interventions. HHVBP reviewers distinguish between agencies that accurately identify high-risk patients and manage them well versus agencies with genuinely high fall rates due to poor prevention. Accurate high-risk identification is clinically appropriate and supports better outcomes.

items.question: Can we use telemedicine for falls risk assessment?
items.answer: Telemedicine can support assessment (video gait evaluation, questionnaire completion), but physical examination is preferable for balance and function testing. Able Assess supports hybrid models: video components (gait, transfers) plus clinician questions. Ensure your telemedicine assessment is comprehensive and properly documented for OASIS coding.

items.question: How does medication review tie into HHVBP falls measures?
items.answer: Medication review is part of good falls prevention but is not directly measured by HHVBP. However, identifying high-risk medication patterns (psychoactive drugs, polypharmacy) during assessment should trigger care plan interventions (pharmacy consultation, prescriber communication). Document these interventions; they support your quality narrative.

items.question: What if our EHR doesn't allow OASIS integration?
items.answer: Many older EHR systems require manual OASIS data entry. This creates delays and errors. Work with your EHR vendor to improve integration or consider switching to systems with built-in OASIS modules. Able Assess can export assessment data in OASIS-compatible formats, reducing manual entry and improving accuracy.

blocks.heading: Ensure HHVBP compliance and secure bonus payments
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess guides your team through OASIS-compliant falls risk assessment, automatically populates OASIS codes, and tracks outcomes to position your agency for HHVBP bonuses.
